chapters.title: Learning never really ends
chapters.text:
A strange idea can turn out to be a great one. With math, prior knowledge, and a few tries, you can make a smart guess about what will happen if you test it.

NASA wanted to land the Curiosity rover on Mars without crashing it or kicking up dust that would block the view, since a clear view was key. A once-ridiculous idea, the sky crane, was the answer. The crane hovered high above the surface, fired by rockets, and lowered the rover on a long tether. Because the rockets stayed high, they did not throw dust onto the landing zone. A parachute would not have worked because the rover was too heavy.

In life, we learn from other people or through trial and error.

This summary covers uncommon ways to do everyday things, like jumping, throwing a party, digging a hole, listening to music, crossing a river, throwing objects, playing football, predicting the weather, skiing, making power, taking a selfie, winning an election, and charging a phone. You probably know how to do most of these. The ideas here may help you do them in new ways. And if you do not know how, you are one of about ten thousand Americans learning something new today.

chapters.title: You can learn to jump much higher than you think
chapters.text:
A well-trained athlete can usually jump a little over two feet without help. With a pole vault, using a springy pole and a running start, the bar can reach about twenty feet.

To jump higher, you can lean on the air itself.

Runners move faster with a tailwind pushing them from behind. If you time your leap with an updraft, that rising air can lift you a little more. To catch an updraft, the wind has to be faster than your falling speed.

In free fall, most people hit a speed of about 130 miles per hour. With a sleek suit, you can slow that to about 30 miles per hour. That is still too fast to ride the wind up, unless you use a parachute or a paraglider.

A suit shaped like a sailplane, used with mountain wave winds, can help you jump higher.

Mountain air usually climbs a short way into the sky. In some places and weather, the wave can reach the stratosphere. With a glider suit, a leap from a peak, and the right ridge lift, you might ride the wind up into the stratosphere and outclimb any plane. You would hold the world record for the highest jump. Best to leave this to the pros.

chapters.title: A pool party needs actual water
chapters.text:
A pool can be in the ground or above it. An in-ground pool needs a hole about twenty feet by thirty feet by five feet deep, lined with plastic or rubber. If you do not have time for that, an above-ground pool is easier. The main concern is hoop stress. Hoop stress is the sideways push on the pool walls from the weight of the water and the ground below it.

If the wall is weaker than the hoop stress, the pool will burst.

To find the hoop stress, multiply water depth by water density by gravity. Then multiply that by the ratio of pool radius to wall thickness. You can rearrange the math to find any missing number. For example, wood is weaker than aluminum in raw strength, but a thick wood wall can hold more water than a thin aluminum one.

A good choice for an above-ground pool is fiberglass. Its strength is about 150 megapascals.

With that kind of strength, a wall only millimeters thick can hold the water pressure.

A pool party needs a pool full of water, and you have a few ways to fill it. A medium pool needs about twenty thousand gallons. Where will the water come from? You could buy bottled water online. A same-day shipment of twenty-four packs of premium water would cost around a quarter million dollars. With 150,000 bottles and only 86,400 seconds in a day, you would still run out of time, even if you opened one per second. A better plan is to use a 15,000-watt industrial shredder. You could fill the pool in just over two hours.

During the Cold War in 1955, the US Federal Civil Defense Administration set off small nuclear tests on beer, soda, and carbonated water.

chapters.title: Picking a smart way to dig
chapters.text:
When you think of digging, the first tool is usually a shovel. The size of the hole may force you to rethink that. A shovel moves about a third to one full cubic meter of dirt per hour. A laborer in the US earns about eighteen dollars an hour. If you are digging for buried treasure, make sure the find is worth more than the work and that the map is real.

There is only one well-recorded pirate treasure in history, but the idea of buried gold became popular after Robert Louis Stevenson wrote Treasure Island. Archaeologists and builders dig too, but treasure hunters are the most common diggers, even if the loot is rare.

The most valuable single piece of traditional treasure in the world is a 12-gram pink diamond called the Pink Star, sold in 2017 for about 71 million dollars.

Let us say you find something worth digging for. Now you need a plan.

For wide and deep holes, a good method is to combine a strong vacuum with a jet of high-pressure air or water.

A vacuum setup can dig about ten times faster. It also avoids damage to pipes, roots, and any buried treasure.

We should also think about the harm a hole can do. In 1590, a Spanish priest named Jose de Acosta warned that connecting the oceans across land could bring not just environmental harm but also spiritual harm, three centuries before the Panama Canal was built.

In 1699, the Scottish pirate William Kidd buried gold and silver on Gardiners Island just before his arrest. The owner of the island, John Gardiner, gave it to the crown once Kidd was caught and executed.

chapters.title: There are sounds a piano cannot reach
chapters.text:
A standard piano has 88 keys. Its notes run from about 27 hertz to 4,186 hertz. A healthy human ear hears from 20 hertz to 20,000 hertz. To cover the full range of human hearing, a piano would need 120 keys. What if we also want notes above and below our hearing?

Sounds above 20,000 hertz are called ultrasound. Sounds below 20 hertz are called infrasound. Both are silent to us.

Ever wonder how a dog whistle works? You cannot hear it, but your dog can. It plays a note too high for human ears. Dogs can hear up to 40 kilohertz. Dogs, cats, bats, and elephants can hear well above or below our range.

Higher sounds do not travel as far in the air.

Ultrasound travels better in water and solids, which is how electric toothbrushes, medical scans, and the echolocation of whales and dolphins work.

Around 150 kilohertz is a useful limit for ultrasound in air because the sound can travel a few dozen meters. Bats can hear that pitch. A piano for bat music would need 155 keys.

Elephants can hear sounds as low as 15 hertz. To play music for them, you would need five extra keys on the far left.

Ultrasound fades faster than normal sound, but infrasound carries farther.

Nuclear blasts, big ocean waves, and huge machines all make infrasound. You need a tool like a spectrogram to pick it up. Ocean waves make a sound near 0.2 hertz, called microbaroms. To play it, a piano would need 235 keys.

A piano that size is hard to build. A rotary woofer for infrasound and a piezo transducer for ultrasound can do the job instead.

chapters.title: Throwing is more of a skill than people think
chapters.text:
There is a famous story about George Washington throwing a silver dollar across a river. We do not really know the physics of throwing. We do know the object flies through the air.

Throwing means using a small space to launch an object with the muscles of the body. A 60-kilogram athlete can put out up to 1,200 watts of power in a throw.

You can guess how far a throw will go if you know the shape, weight, and drag of the object.

There is a calculator at xkcd.com/throw that will show you your range for different objects.

A spinning object has less drag than a tumbling one. For Washington’s silver dollar, a spin would have carried it about 467 feet. A tumble would cut that to around 167 feet because of the extra drag. No one knows the truth, since the story grew up after he died.

Do you need to throw things? Basic physics can help you guess your range.

In 1936, first baseman Lou Gehrig threw a silver dollar across about 400 feet of the Hudson River. The pitcher Walter Johnson threw one across the 386-foot-wide Rappahannock. They must have used a spin.

chapters.title: Football tricks for scoring a goal
chapters.text:
Many sports are called football. Indoor soccer, foosball, American football, arena football, Paralympic football, rugby, association football, and Gaelic football are some of them. They share a ball, players on a field, and the goal of putting that ball into the other team’s net. The size and shape of the ball depend on the game.

In some games, only certain body parts can touch the ball. In American football, the quarterback handles the ball more than anyone else. Some games let you kick or throw from far out, but in American football, the quarterback cannot throw the ball straight through the goal. In association football, the goalie can throw the ball into the other team’s net. To pull that off, the goalie needs a great throwing arm to beat the other keeper.

More weight, more speed, and more power can help you charge toward the goal.

One way to get an edge is to ride a very large horse and claim there is no rule against it. To see if your horse can push through the other team, you can use the drag equation from physics.

Drag equals half the drag coefficient times air density times the cross-section area times the square of speed.

Faster motion meets more drag. If you double the speed, the drag force goes up four times. To beat that drag, you need more power. Power equals drag times speed.

A horse can put out ten to twenty horsepower in a short burst, and one horsepower is about 746 watts.

chapters.title: A few final thoughts
chapters.text:
You have just seen some wild ways to do ordinary things. Each one is grounded in real science. The goal has been to stretch your thinking by showing strange but accurate ways to score a goal, throw objects, build a piano that can play sounds we cannot hear, dig a hole, set up a pool, and jump much higher than is humanly normal.

This summary has been a mix of math, science, and history. You have seen great feats pulled off by athletes, politicians, and odd thinkers. The big lesson is that we all need to think in fresh ways, because that is how the best ideas appear. The plane, the smartphone, space travel, the internet, and the computer all started as strange ideas that changed the world.

Most of these ideas are bad in practice, so please do not try them. But they open our minds to the fact that there is almost always more than one way to do something. That kind of thinking leads to new ideas and useful discoveries. The world can never get enough of this kind of thinking. It drives human progress.

Try this. You can dig a hole without shoveling. Use a vacuum cleaner and a jet of air or water. See how deep you can go with those tools. It is a bad idea, but you never know what you might find.
